segments.s449-g81: جهاندار پیروز is a titled noun phrase where جهاندار (literally 'world-holder') is the head and پیروز is an epithet placed after it ('victorious'). با دیده is an instrumental phrase ('with his eye') formed by preposition با + noun دیده, and گفت is the past tense 3rd-person singular of گفتن ('he said'); together با دیده گفت idiomatically means 'he signaled/communicated with his eye' rather than a full spoken sentence.
segments.s449-g82: که introduces a subordinate content clause ('that'). چیزی که دیدی is a noun with a relative clause (چیزی 'something' + که 'that/which' + دیدی 'you saw' — 2nd person past). بباید is the classical modal auxiliary ('it must/ought') with the archaic prefixal form, and نهفت is the lexical verb/adjectival form meaning 'be hidden' or 'to hide' — here functioning as the predicate after the modal, so the clause means 'that something which you saw must be hidden.'
segments.s449-g1: اندر introduces an adverbial phrase 'به بیماری اندر' (literally 'by/through illness') that modifies the verb بمرد. بمرد is an archaic/poetic verb form used here as a past event ('died'); the proper name اردشیر follows the verb in this inverted, poetic word order so the whole segment means 'Ardashir died of illness.'
segments.s449-g2: همی + بود is a classical progressive/static past construction meaning 'was/stayed (in a state)'; بی‌کار is a predicate adjective 'unoccupied/idle' that applies to the coordinated noun phrase تاج و سریر ('crown and throne'). The sequence thus states the continuing condition: 'the crown and throne were left unoccupied.'
segments.s449-g3: همای آمد is a simple clause 'the Homa came.' The second clause uses the object-first poetic order: تاج (the crown) + بر سر (on the head) + نهاد (past of نهادن 'to place'), so نهاد functions as a past tense verb 'placed/put.' Together: 'and (he) placed the crown on (his) head.'
segments.s449-g4: یکی qualifies the first element (راه) as 'one (way)' and آیین follows as the second coordinated object introduced by و. The verb نهاد is repeated or implied at the end and means 'established/put in place.' The clause reads: 'He set one way and another custom (in place),' i.e. he instituted differing policies or practices.
segments.s449-g5: سپه را marks 'the army' as the definite object (را). همه سربسر is an emphatic adverbial phrase 'all, entirely' modifying the verb phrase. بار داد here literally 'gave a load' is used idiomatically: he outfitted or loaded the army (i.e., supplied or bestowed on them). داد is the simple past 'gave.'
segments.s449-g6: در گنج is a locative phrase 'into/within the treasure (vault).' بگشاد is a past form 'opened' (classical b‑ + verb stem), so 'he opened the treasury,' and the coordinated clause و دینار داد follows with دینار 'coins' and داد 'gave' — 'and gave dinars.'
segments.s449-g7: The repeated preposition به before رای و به داد is an older/poetic marking of the two qualities 'counsel and justice.' از پدر برگذشت uses برگذشت (past of برگذشتن) meaning 'passed/transferred (from)' — a way of saying these virtues came to him from his father ('counsel and justice passed from his father').
segments.s449-g8: همی again gives a continuous/emphatic sense ('thus/indeed'). گیتی 'the world' is the subject; از introduces the causal agent 'from/by his justice' (دادش = 'his justice'). آباد گشت is a common classical predicate 'became prosperous/inhabited.' So the clause means 'the world became prosperous because of his justice.'
segments.s449-g49: به زیر اندرش is a prepositional phrase: به + زیر (beneath) + اندر + -ش (enclitic ‘his/its’). اندر is an archaic/poetic preposition meaning “in/within,” here combined with the possessive -ش to yield “beneath/under it/him.” بستر خواب کرد is literal “made a bed for sleep” (بستر = bed/bedding, خواب = sleep) with کرد as the past tense verb “made/did,” so the segment means “they placed/made a bed beneath it for sleeping.”
segments.s449-g50: میانش is میان + -ش, “its/midst.” پر از is the fixed phrase “full of.” در here functions as internal/inside (poetic use) and is governed by پر از, so در خوشاب reads “full of خوشاب” (خوشاب = pleasant or sweet water/liqueur or fragrant liquid). کرد is the past verb “they made/put/filled.” The whole clause means “they filled its interior/midst with pleasant water.”
segments.s449-g51: بسی here is a poetic quantifier meaning “much” or “many.” زر سرخ is the noun + adjective order common in Classical Persian: “gold (زر) red (سرخ)” — i.e. red gold. اندرو is an archaic/inverted form meaning “within/inside it” (اندر + -او/ -ش), and ریخته is a past participle/stantial past meaning “scattered/laid/poured” used predicatively: “much red gold was scattered inside.”
segments.s449-g52: عقیق و زبرجد are two gem nouns joined by و (“and”). برآمیخته is a compound past/adjective (بر- prefix + آمیخته “mixed”), meaning “mingled” or “blended together.” Word order places the list of gems before the participial adjective: “agate and peridot mingled (with it).”
segments.s449-g53: ببستند is the past tense verb “they bound/tied/fastened.” بس functions as a quantifier (“many/abundant” or “much” in older usage) preceding the noun گوهر (jewel). شاهوار is an adjective meaning “kingly/regal,” following the noun in the classical Persian order, so the phrase reads “they fastened many regal jewels.”
segments.s449-g54: به introduces the indirect object/target “to/on.” بازوی is the oblique/construct form of بازو (arm) and here marks the place where the jewels were fastened. آن کودک شیرخوار is the noun phrase “that child, a suckling/infant,” with شیرخوار as a postpositive attribute. The whole segment literally: “to the arm of that suckling child.”
segments.s449-g55: بدانگه is an adverb meaning “thereupon” or “at that moment.” که introduces a subordinate clause (“when/that”), and شد is the past of شدن “became.” کودک is the subject “the child,” and از خواب مست is a figurative expression: از + خواب + مست = “intoxicated/by sleep,” i.e. fast/asleep or enraptured in sleep. Grammatically this is a temporal clause: “When the child became drunk (deeply) with sleep…”
segments.s449-g56: خروشان is an adverbial/participial adjective meaning “roaring/turbulent/noisy.” بشد (بـ + شد) here is the past “it became / was made” (i.e. “became noisy”). The noun دایه (nurse) follows and is qualified by the compound adjective چرب دست (“oily/greasy‑handed”), where چرب modifies دست to yield the idiom “greasy‑handed.” Classical Persian often inverts subject and predicate for poetic effect, so the clause reads “the greasy‑handed nurse became clamorous/noisy.”
segments.s449-g73: This clause is a simple SOV past clause typical of classical Persian: 'یکی گازر' (a certain passerby) is the subject, 'آن خرد صندوق' (that small chest) is the object, and 'دید' is the past tense 3rd‑person singular verb 'saw'. 'یکی' is an indefinite marker here ('a certain'), and 'خرد' is an adjective after the demonstrative 'آن' describing the chest.
segments.s449-g74: This coordinate clause continues in the past tense. 'بپویید' is a classical/prefixed past form meaning 'he went/approached' (b‑ + verb root), 'وز' functions here as a poetic preposition equivalent to 'از' (from), and 'کارگه برکشید' is a transitive past clause 'he lifted/drew up from the workshop' (برکشید = past 3rd‑person of برکشیدن).
segments.s449-g75: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'. 'بگشاد' is an archaic prefixed past form of 'گشودن' ('he opened'), and 'گسترده‌ها' (plural with the enclitic plural -ها) means 'the spread out things/cloths'. 'برگرفت' is the main past verb 'he took up/collected' and agrees with the preceding object.
segments.s449-g76: 'بماند' is past tense 'he remained/stayed'. 'اندران کار' is a locative phrase 'in/at that work' (اندران = therein/among them + کار = matter/deed), and 'گازر شگفت' places the subject 'the passer' with the adjective 'شگفت' (astonished/wondering) — the sentence omits a copula, so the meaning is 'the passer remained there, amazed at that deed.'
segments.s449-g77: 'به جامه بپوشید' is a past action 'he put on a garment' (به + object + بپوشید). The conjunction 'و' links the next action 'آمد' ('he came'). 'دمان' here follows 'آمد' as an adverbial/nominal detail referring to the skirt/hem of the robe (i.e. his trailing garment) — in older Persian such clothing details often accompany motion verbs to describe manner.
segments.s449-g78: A series of predicates/adjectives describing his appearance or mood: 'پرامید' (hopeful), 'شادان' (joyful), 'روشن‑روان' (bright‑soul'd/cheerful). Classical Persian commonly lists adjectives without a copula; they all modify the subject of the previous clause ('he').
segments.s449-g79: 'سبک' is used adjectivally (here 'light' = nimble) modifying 'دیده‌بان' (the sentinel/watchman). 'پیش مامش' means 'to/into the presence of his mother' (پیش used for direction or 'before/in front of' someone), and 'دوید' is the past 'he ran'. Word order places the adjective before the noun, then the prepositional phrase, then the verb.
segments.s449-g80: 'ز صندوق' ('from the chest') is a prepositional phrase introducing the source. The coordinated noun phrase 'و گازر' places 'the passer' as the agent; 'بگفت' is the past 'he said/told', and 'آنچ دید' is a fused relative phrase 'that which he saw' (آنچ = 'that which' + دید = 'he saw'), functioning as the content of what was told.
segments.s449-g17: This is a noun phrase with a relative clause: مهان جهان را ("the world's guests") is followed by که دارند گنج ("who have treasure"). که introduces the relative clause; دارند is the present/plural form of داشتن "to have/possess" and governs the object گنج. را here marks/flags the definite NP (a topical/definite marker common in Classical/Persian poetry).
segments.s449-g18: نداریم is the 1st-person-plural negative present of داشتن (we do not have). زان is a demonstrative/relational particle here (literally "from/of that" → "those"), introducing نیکویها "those good things/virtues." به رنج is a prepositional phrase meaning "by/through hardship"; together the clause means we do not possess those goods except by toil.
segments.s449-g19: چو here is the temporal conjunction "when." هنگام زادنش is a genitival/time expression "the time of his birth" (زادن + enclitic -ش = "his birth"). آمد فراز places the verb آمد "came" after the time phrase; فراز is an adverb/particle here meaning "on high/above" or "up/now" used idiomatically with آمد to mean "came/arrived."
segments.s449-g20: ز شهر و ز لشکر uses the preposition ز (poetic for "from/of" or "with respect to") twice to indicate the groups excluded from/concerned by the secret: "the city and the army." همی داشت راز is a periphrastic past/continuous idea: همی (archaic continuous/emphatic) + داشت (was having/kept) + راز (secret) = "he was keeping a secret" or "he kept the secret from them."
segments.s449-g21: تخت شاهی is a nominal phrase meaning "the royal throne/kingship." پسند آمدش is an impersonal/experiential construction: پسند ("approval/liking") + آمدن ("to come") with the dative/clitic -ش (= "to him") yields "it pleased him" or "he took a liking to it." همی is an older emphatic/continuous particle here adding a literary tone.
segments.s449-g22: جهان داشتن is a verbal-nominal phrase meaning "to have/possess the world" (the infinitival/gerund sense). سودمند آمدش repeats the same experiential pattern: سودمند ("useful/profitable") + آمدن with -ش = "seemed profitable to him" (lit. "profit came to him"). The whole clause states that possessing the world appeared advantageous to him.
segments.s449-g23: نهانی is an adverb/adjective "secretly/hidden." پسر زاد is a compact verbal/nominal phrase where زاد (here a past/nominalized root from زادن) indicates birth/was born — together meaning "a son was born" or "he had a son." و با کس نگفت is a coordinated clause: با کس "to/with anyone" + نگفت (negative past of گفتن) = "he did not tell anyone." Note: نگفت is a verb ("did not say/tell").
segments.s449-g24: همی داشت here again is the continuous/emphatic past idea "he kept/was holding." آن نیکویی is the demonstrative NP "that goodness/that good thing." در نهفت is a prepositional+adjective phrase meaning "kept in concealment/hidden." Together: he kept that good thing concealed.
segments.s449-g25: بیاورد is a classical third-person past/past‑narrative verb meaning “brought” placed before its object (typical in Ferdowsi’s poetic word order). آزاده‌تن is a compound attributive (literally “free-born” / noble) modifying دایه; را is the definite/direct‑object marker attached to دایه, marking “the nurse” as the thing brought.
segments.s449-g26: یکی introduces an appositive noun phrase “one / a certain (one)”; the following adjectives پاک, پرشرم and بامایه sequentially qualify that one (پاک = pure, پرشرم = ‘full of shame’ i.e. modest/bashful, بامایه = ‘with means’ i.e. well‑provided/wealthy). The final را repeats the object marker for emphasis/clarity, echoing the previous direct object.
segments.s449-g27: نهانی is an adverb “secretly/covertly” modifying the verb داد (gave). بدو is the indirect object “to him/her” placed before the verb; فرزند is the direct object and is marked by را. The clause thus reads “secretly gave the child to her/him.”
segments.s449-g28: چنان introduces an appositive description of the previously mentioned child (“such a…”) and is followed by the descriptive noun phrase شاه شاخ برومند; شاخ here is used metaphorically as ‘branch’/lineage and برومند is a postpositive adjective meaning flourishing/prosperous. The trailing را again echoes the object marking, linking this descriptive apposition back to فرزند را.
segments.s449-g29: کسی کو is an indefinite/relative phrase meaning “whenever anyone / someone who”; here it introduces the conditional context. ز + فرزند او literally “from his child” means “about/from his offspring” and نام برد is the verbal phrase “to mention/take a name” (i.e. to speak the child’s name). Word order places the agentive/indefinite phrase first, then the prepositional phrase, then the verbal phrase.
segments.s449-g30: چنین گفت is a formulaic reporting phrase “they would say thus”; کان here introduces the reported clause ('that' / ‘that which’) in an archaic register. پاک‌زاده is the reported noun “the noble‑born one,” and بمرد is the verbal form used in reported speech meaning ‘(that) he died’ (an older/subjunctive-like form used here to report people’s words that he was dead).
segments.s449-g31: همان is the demonstrative “that/the very,” modifying تاج شاهی “the royal crown.” The prepositional phrase به سر (“onto the head”) precedes the verb; نهاد is the past form of نهادن “to put/place” (here ‘placed’). The verb comes at the end, following classical Persian/poetic word order.
segments.s449-g32: همی بود is a habitual/continuous past construction “he was / he remained” (همی gives continued aspect). بر تخت is the locative “on the throne,” and پیروز و شاد are predicate adjectives “victorious and joyful” that describe his state while on the throne.
segments.s449-g9: نخستین ("at first") is a temporal adverb introducing a time clause; که here is the subordinating conjunction 'that/when' linking this clause to what follows. دیهیم is an archaic/poetic noun here (not matching the supplied senses) referring to the royal diadem/crown; بر + سر + نهاد is the verb phrase 'placed on the head', where نهاد is the past of نهادن ('put/place') and بر سر is the locative 'upon the head'.
segments.s449-g10: جهان را is the definite object ('the world' + the object marker را). به here introduces the manner/quality ('by/with' or 'in the matter of') and links to the nouns داد ('justice') and دهش ('giving, bounty'); مژده داد is the main verb phrase 'gave (the) glad tidings', where the final داد is the past tense verb 'gave'.
segments.s449-g11: که introduces the reported content (the announced blessing). این تاج و این تخت lists the two items 'this crown and this throne.' فرخنده باد is an optative/formulaic blessing — باد here is the optative particle meaning 'may it be' and makes 'فرخنده' ('auspicious/fortunate') into a wish.
segments.s449-g12: دل بدسگالان ما is a genitive/possessive phrase 'the hearts of our bad‑omened/ill‑wishing ones' (بدسگالان = ill‑omened / ill‑wishing people). کنده باد uses the past participle کنده ('torn/uprooted') plus the optative باد to express a wish — 'may ... be torn away.' The structure is an optative passive wish concerning the hearts of those enemies.
segments.s449-g13: همه نیکویی باد is a wish-clause where همه نیکویی ('all goodness') is the predicative content and باد is the optative 'may it be'; کردار ما ('our conduct') is the logical subject complement ('may all goodness be our conduct'). Word order places the wished quality before the noun it is ascribed to.
segments.s449-g14: مبیناد is an optative/subjunctive form meaning essentially 'may (it) not appear/let it not be seen' or 'may no one see'; کس is 'anyone/person' and رنج و تیمار ما is the object phrase 'our suffering and care.' Grammatically this is another wish: 'may no one witness/meet our pain and solicitude.'
segments.s449-g15: توانگر کنیم is a causative/active clause 'we make (someone) rich' (کنیم here = 'we make' / 'we cause'). آنک is a relative pronoun 'the one who' linking to the following clause; درویش بود is a relative clause 'was a dervish/poor man' describing that person. Together: 'we make rich him who was poor.'
segments.s449-g16: نیازش is 'his need' (subject), به رنج تن خویش is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by/through the toil/suffering of his body' (خویش = his own, here split into خوی + ش), and بود is the copula 'was.' The clause states a past fact about him: his need came from the hardship of his own body.
segments.s449-g65: پس‌اندر is an adverbial phrase 'afterwards' placed up front. همی رفت is the epic past-continuous construction ('were going' / 'set off'), with همی marking continuing action and رفت the past verb 'went'. پویان is a participial/adjectival modifier ('in pursuit; active') immediately before the head noun دو مرد, so the literal order is 'in pursuit two men' (poetic inversion).
segments.s449-g66: که introduces a subordinate clause (here an indirect interrogative/purpose clause). تا has the purposive sense 'so that / in order to' and together set up 'to see/learn'. آب با شیرخواره is a prepositional/nominal phrase 'at/by the water with the suckling/infant' (با = 'with'); چه کرد is the interrogative + past verb 'what did (he/they) do'.
segments.s449-g67: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. کشتی همی رفت is a past-continuous clause 'the boat was moving/on its way' (همی + رفت). چوب اندر آب is a noun phrase 'a log in the water' placed after the verb by poetic inversion and functions as the item that will be taken in the next clause.
segments.s449-g68: نگهبان is the agent 'the guard'. آنرا is the object pronoun with را marking ('that/it'). گرفته is a past participle/verb 'having seized' or simple past 'seized', and شتاب here expresses quick motion—used verbally as 'hastened' (literal 'took it and hurried'). The sequence gives 'the guard seized it and hurried away.'
segments.s449-g69: سپیده 'dawn' is personified; چو introduces the temporal clause 'when'. برزد is an archaic verbal form meaning 'came/rose' (here 'lifted/appeared'), and سر 'head' with از کوهسار 'from the mountain ridge' yields the idiom 'dawn raised its head from the mountains.'
segments.s449-g70: بگردید is an older/poetic intransitive verb meaning 'turned up / appeared / was found' and is placed before its noun (verb–subject inversion typical of poetry). صندوق is the subject 'the chest', and بر رودبار is the locative phrase 'on the riverbank' (بر = 'on/upon').
segments.s449-g71: به + گازرگهی marks location 'at the mountain pass'. کاندرو is an appositive/adjectival idea ('a beehive / honey-store') describing the stone; poetic word order places بود (was) before the noun سنگ (stone), so the clause reads 'at the pass there was a kandru-stone.'
segments.s449-g72: سر جوی را is the object phrase 'the head/top of the stream' with را marking. کارگه کرده is a causative/perfect phrase 'had made (into) a workshop/workplace'—here metaphorically 'turned into a confined channel'—and تنگ is the adjective 'narrow', placed after the verb phrase to qualify the effected state.
segments.s449-g33: ز is the poetic equivalent of از (’from’ / ’because of’). بهر سو که is a relative-directional phrase: بهر سو = 'to/for each direction' and که introduces a relative clause 'where/which'. بد here modifies مهتری and has the sense 'hostile / unfavorable', so بد مهتری is an adjectival phrase meaning something like 'hostile supremacy / hostile preeminence' (an old/formal compound); the whole segment sets the circumstance/motive that links into the verb in the next segment.
segments.s449-g34: فرستاد is simple past 3rd-person 'he sent/dispatch(ed)'. بر هر سوی لشکری is a prepositional/direct-object cluster: بر = 'to/against/upon', هر سوی = 'each quarter/side', لشکری (adjectival/nominal) = 'an army / troops' — together 'he sent troops to every quarter.' The subject is omitted (typical Persian) and picked up from context.
segments.s449-g35: ز introduces the source 'from'. چیزی که is a free relative 'that which / what', and رفتی is a past verb 'had gone / had been' (an archaic/poetic past form here meaning 'he had gone' or 'he had travelled'); به گرد جهان is a circumstantial phrase 'around/throughout the world' — together: 'from what he had experienced in travelling the world'.
segments.s449-g36: نبودی functions impersonally to indicate non-existence ('there was not' / 'did not remain'), so the clause reads 'no bad and good from him was hidden'. بد و نیک = 'bad and good' (i.e. evil and goodness), ازو = 'from him / concerning him', در نهان = 'in hiddenness / hidden'. Word order places the negated existential verb up front, a common poetic ordering: 'no good or evil of him remained hidden.'
segments.s449-g37: به گیتی sets the domain/topic 'in the world'. به جز = 'except / but', so به جز داد و نیکی نخواست is a negative desiderative pattern: نخواست ('he did not want') with the preceding phrase means 'he wanted nothing except justice and goodness' (i.e. 'he desired only justice and goodness').
segments.s449-g38: جهان را uses را as the definite/object marker ('the world'). سراسر = 'throughout / wholly'. همی داشت is a habitual/progressive past construction ('he kept / he maintained') — here it means he maintained the world in a certain state. راست is an adjective 'right / just', so the phrase means 'he kept the world wholly right/just.'
segments.s449-g39: جهانی شده is a passive/resultative construction 'the world had become' or 'the world was made'. ایمن از indicates 'secure/safe by/through' with از marking the cause or means, so ایمن از داد او = 'secure through his justice' (i.e. his justice made the world safe). او = 'his'.
segments.s449-g40: به کشور is locative 'in the realm/kingdom'. نبودی again gives an existential negative 'there was not' / 'there remained not'. به جز یاد او = 'except his memory/renown' — the whole line: 'in the realm there was nothing except his remembrance' (i.e. his fame alone remained).
segments.s449-g41: بدین سان (here written as ب + دین + سان) is a fixed adverbial 'in this manner/thus'. همی بود is a past continuous/habitual construction (همی + بود) meaning 'kept being/continued to be'. تا هشت ماه marks the temporal extent 'for/until eight months' (تا used as 'until/for' here).
segments.s449-g42: پسر گشت is 'the son became' with گشت as the past of گشتن used here in the 'become' sense. ماننده is a simile marker 'like/similar to' and modifies what follows. رفته شاه is a compound 'the gone/departed king' (رفته = departed, شاه = king), i.e. 'the late king.'
segments.s449-g43: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb 'he commanded/said' used to introduce an order. تا introduces the subordinate/purpose clause ('that/so that'). درگری (درگر) means 'another/different one' and پاک‌مغز is a compound adjective 'pure‑minded' describing that person; together درگری پاک‌مغز = 'another of pure mind.'
segments.s449-g44: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain/one'. تخته 'plank/board' is the object, and جست is the past of جستن used in the sense 'to leap/spring'. از در is a spatial phrase 'out of/from the door' (از = from; در = door), and کار نغز is a nominative qualifier 'a clever/witty deed' describing how the action was done.
segments.s449-g45: یکی 'a certain' introduces the noun phrase. خرد here means 'small' modifying صندوق 'chest/box'. از چوب خشک is a material phrase 'from dry wood' (از = from; چوب = wood; خشک = dry). Word order is poetic but standard: numeral/indefinite + adjective + noun + material phrase.
segments.s449-g46: بکردند is the past plural 'they made/did'. و is the coordinating 'and/then'. برزد is an archaic poetic verb here meaning they applied/covered (Shahnameh uses such forms; the exact nuance is archaic). برو appears here as part of the phrase that governs the following nouns and signals the material applied 'with/on' (poetic usage). قیر و مشک are the materials 'tar (pitch) and musk' that they used to coat the chest.
segments.s449-g47: درون نرم کرده is a participial phrase 'having made the inside soft' (درون = inside; نرم = soft; کرده is the perfect participle 'made/caused'). به دیبای روم specifies the material/instrument 'with Roman brocade' (به + noun indicating the covering or lining).
segments.s449-g48: براندوده here is a past participle used in the sense 'covered/wrapped' (poetic usage; literal dictionary senses given in the list don't fully capture this poetically extended sense). بیرون او means 'its outside' (بیرون = outside; او = it/him referring to the chest). مشک و موم are the substances 'musk and wax' used to cover it.
segments.s449-g57: نهادش is the past verb نهاد + object suffix -ش (‚they placed him/it"). The phrase به صندوق در uses به + صندوق + در to indicate placement into the chest (به…در = ‘into the chest’), and نرم نرم is a reduplicated adverbial (soft/gently) used for emphasis; word order places the verb before the locative phrase and the manner adverb at the end, as common in epic Persian.
segments.s449-g58: به چینی is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in Chinese’ (here ‘in Chinese cloth/garment’). پرندش is a noun with the pronominal suffix -ش (‘his/him’) that names what they wrapped/covered (the exact lexical sense is archaic/obscure in this line). بپوشید is a past, transitive form meaning ‘they covered/wrapped’ (an older epic form without an explicit -ند), and گرم qualifies the covering as warm/for warmth.
segments.s449-g59: سر تنگ تابوت is a noun phrase meaning ‘the (narrow) head/mouth of the coffin’ (سر here = top/upper part; تنگ = narrow/close). کردند خشک is the periphrastic past ‘they made/made it dry’ (کردند + adjective خشک), i.e., they dried/sealed it.
segments.s449-g60: A series introduced by repeated به marks the substances used to seal: دبق (a sticky/adhesive substance), عنبر (ambergris), قیر (tar/pitch) and مشک (musk). The coordinating و links items in the list; together they indicate how the coffin was sealed.
segments.s449-g61: ببردند is past plural ‘they took/carried away’. صندوق را uses the object marker را after صندوق to mark it as definite object. نیم شب is a temporal compound (‘half-night’ = midnight) placed after the verb to indicate when the action took place.
segments.s449-g62: یکی بر دگر نیز is an idiomatic pairing ‘one and the other (of the two) as well’; نیز = ‘also/too’. نگشاد لب is a negative past construction (ن + گشاد, from گشودن ‘to open’) applied to لب (‘lip’), i.e. ‘did not open his lips’ meaning ‘spoke not a word’. Together it says that neither of the two spoke.
segments.s449-g63: ز پیش همایش combines the poetic preposition ز (from) + پیش (before) with همایش (‘the assembly/gathering’) to mean ‘from before the assembly’. برون is the adverb ‘out/forth’, and تاختند is past plural ‘they rushed/galloped’ — together ‘they rushed out from before the assembly.’
segments.s449-g64: به آب فرات اندر انداختند places the action into its goal: به آب فرات = ‘into the water of the Euphrates’ (به… used with a body of water) and اندر = ‘into/within’; انداختند is the past plural ‘they threw/cast’. The object (the chest/child) is implied from previous clauses.

sentences.s449-s41: The victorious sovereign signaled with his eye, saying that whatever you saw must be hidden.
sentences.s449-s1: Ardashir died of an illness, and the crown and throne remained unoccupied.
sentences.s449-s2: The Homa came and set the crown upon the head; he established one way and ordained another practice.
sentences.s449-s3: He equipped (or burdened) the whole army completely, opened the treasure, and gave dinars.
sentences.s449-s4: Counsel and justice passed down from his father; by his justice the world became prosperous.
sentences.s449-s25: They laid a sleeping bed beneath it and filled its interior with pleasant (fragrant) water.
sentences.s449-s26: A great quantity of red gold lay inside, mingled with agate and peridot.
sentences.s449-s27: They fastened many regal jewels to the arm of that suckling child.
sentences.s449-s28: Then, when the child became intoxicated with sleep, the greasy‑handed nurse grew loud and tumultuous.
sentences.s449-s37: A certain passerby saw that small chest, went up to it, and lifted it out of the workshop.
sentences.s449-s38: When he opened it, he took up the spread‑out things; he remained there, astonished at that work.
sentences.s449-s39: He put on a robe and came, hopeful, joyful, and of bright spirit.
sentences.s449-s40: Light‑footed the watchman ran to his mother; from the chest the passer told what he had seen.
sentences.s449-s9: The world's guests who possess treasure — we obtain those good things only through hardship.
sentences.s449-s10: When the time of his birth came, he kept the secret from the city and the army.
sentences.s449-s11: The royal throne pleased him; possessing the world seemed profitable to him.
sentences.s449-s12: He secretly had a son and told no one; he kept that good thing hidden.
sentences.s449-s13: He brought a noble-born nurse — one pure, modest, and well-provided.
sentences.s449-s14: He secretly gave the child to her — such a prince, of flourishing stock.
sentences.s449-s15: Whenever anyone mentioned his name, they would say: ‘That noble-born one has died.’
sentences.s449-s16: He placed that royal crown upon his head; he remained on the throne victorious and joyful.
sentences.s449-s5: When, at the first, he placed the diadem on his head, he gave the world the glad tidings of justice and bounty.
sentences.s449-s6: ‘May this crown and this throne be auspicious; may the hearts of our ill-wishers be torn away.’
sentences.s449-s7: May all goodness be our conduct; may no one behold our suffering and care.
sentences.s449-s8: We make rich the one who was a dervish (poor); his need was satisfied by the toil of his own body.
sentences.s449-s33: After that, two men went off in pursuit to find out what had been done with the infant at the water.
sentences.s449-s34: When the boat moved on, the guard seized a log in the water and hastened off with it.
sentences.s449-s35: When dawn lifted its head from the mountains, the chest appeared on the riverbank.
sentences.s449-s36: At a mountain pass there was a beehive-like stone; it had narrowed the head of the stream into a confined channel.
sentences.s449-s17: Against the enemy on every side where hostile supremacy appeared, he dispatched troops to every quarter.
sentences.s449-s18: From what he had seen wandering the world, no good or ill concerning him remained hidden.
sentences.s449-s19: In the world he desired nothing but justice and goodness; he kept the world wholly right.
sentences.s449-s20: The world had become secure through his justice; in the realm there was nothing but his memory.
sentences.s449-s21: Thus it continued for eight months, and the son became like the departed king.
sentences.s449-s22: He commanded that another, pure‑minded man be (sent); one sprang onto a plank from the doorway in a clever act.
sentences.s449-s23: They made a small chest of dry wood, and then they smeared/covered it with pitch and musk.
sentences.s449-s24: They lined the inside with soft Roman brocade and wrapped the outside of it with musk and wax.
sentences.s449-s29: They laid him gently into the chest and wrapped him warmly in Chinese cloth.
sentences.s449-s30: They sealed the coffin's narrow mouth (or head) dry, with dabq and ambergris, with tar and with musk.
sentences.s449-s31: They carried the chest away at midnight; neither of the two opened his lips.
sentences.s449-s32: They rushed out from before that assembly and cast it into the water of the Euphrates.
